major: Chicana/o and Latina/o Studies, Lower Division B.A.

courses.title: Psychology of Latinos/Chicanos in the U.S.
Introduction to Chicano Literature in English
History of the United States: A Mexican American Perspective

notes: COLLEGE ADMISSIONS REQUIREMENTS FOR TRANSFER STUDENTS THIS MAJOR IS OFFERED BY THE COLLEGE OF LETTERS AND SCIENCE (L&S) By the end of the spring term preceding fall enrollment at Berkeley, you must complete: 1) The L&S Essential Skills Requirements: Reading & Composition, Quantitative Reasoning, and Foreign Language; OR 2) IGETC IMPORTANT information for students applying for admission to L&S for the fall 2025 application cycle and beyond Completion of Essential Skills requirements (R&C, QR, and FL) or IGETC will no longer be a requirement for admission; however, completion of the Essential Skills requirements is strongly recommended prior to enrollment at UC Berkeley. For more information on minimum UC eligibility requirements for transfer applicants for fall 2025 and beyond, please visit the UC Berkeley transfer requirements page . Major Requirements: See details on preparation for this major below. Primary selection criteria for admission, in general: - completion of L&S Requirements (or IGETC), plus - strength of academic preparation, and - grade point average. For more information on admission to UC Berkeley: http://admissions.berkeley.edu For more information on majors at UC Berkeley: Berkeley Academic Guide: http://guide.berkeley.edu

The Chicano Studies major offers an interdisciplinary curriculum of academic study that critically examines the historical and contemporary experiences of people of Mexican descent in the context of American society and institutions; and studies particular aspects of Mexican history, culture and politics as they bear upon the Chicano community, past and present. Emphasis is given to the student developing a broad knowledge of the Chicano experience. thus, the major stresses the analysis of the interrelationships in the historical background, cultural patterns and artistic expression of the Chicano community in order to acquire a well-rounded, in-depth understanding of the contemporary interface between Chicanos and American society. In this connection, the major strives to incorporate various disciplines in its approach, such as political science, sociology, anthropology, history, literary criticism and art. Through the interdisciplinary nature of our curriculum, the major is aimed at preparing students for incorporation into the world of work and for a wide range of advanced graduate work and/or professional training in various fields. Please consult with the Chicano Studies adviser regarding the use of courses taken at your community college for credit toward the major. Generally, some UC-transferable courses taken at your community college can in fact be used to meet certain components of the major requirements. For more information contact Major Advisors: Dewey St. Germaine (510) 643-6420 deweystg@berkeley.edu Laura Jimenez-Olvera (510) 642-0243 lauraj@berkeley.edu http://ethnicstudies.berkeley.edu
